8: veasa lecha saraf veshim oto al nes vehaya kol hanashuch veraah oto vachai: 'Make a fiery serpent and set it on a pole, and everyone who is bitten, when he sees it, shall live.' The serpent-on-a-pole raises a question: is this a violation of the second commandment (no graven images)? The text suggests: (1) it is the looking in faith, not the object itself, that saves; (2) YHWH appointed the image for a specific purpose as a means of grace. The Greek translation (ophis chalkous, bronze serpent) uses the same word Paul uses in 2 Corinthians 5:21: God made him who knew no sin to be sin (Gk. hamartia) for us — Christ becomes the thing that kills (sin/the serpent) in order to be the means of salvation for all who look to him.
1: vayishma haKena'ani melech-Arad yoshev haNegev: 'when the Canaanite, the king of Arad, who lived in the Negev, heard.' The first military engagement after Aaron's death: Israel encounters Canaanite opposition from the south. Arad's king hears that Israel is coming by the 'way of Atharim' (perhaps the spies' route from Num 13). The proactive Canaanite attack — taking some prisoners — provokes Israel's vow.
2: vayiddar Yisrael neder laYHWH vayomar im-naton titen et-ha'am hazeh beyadi vehacharamti et-areihem: 'and Israel vowed a vow to YHWH and said: If you will indeed give this people into my hand, then I will devote their cities to destruction [cherem].' Vayiddar neder (vowed a vow) — the cognate construction for emphasis. Cherem (irrevocable devotion/destruction) — total consecration to YHWH of the conquered cities; nothing kept for Israel. The vow conditions military success on YHWH's faithfulness and Israel's total renunciation of spoil. The site is named Chormah (destruction) after the fulfillment (v3).
3: vayishma YHWH bekol Yisrael vayiten et-haKena'ani vayacharem otam ve'et-areihem vayikra shem-hamakom Chormah: 'YHWH heeded the voice of Israel and gave over the Canaanites, and they devoted them and their cities to destruction. So the name of the place was called Hormah.' Vayishma YHWH bekol: YHWH hears the voice of Israel — the prayer is heard. Chormah (from cherem): the place-name preserves the vow's fulfillment. This first land victory after a generation of failure anticipates the coming conquest.
4: vayis'u meihor hahar derech Yam-Suf lisbot et-eretz Edom vatiketzer nefesh ha'am baderech: 'they journeyed from Mount Hor by the way to the Red Sea, to go around the land of Edom. And the people became impatient on the way.' Vatiketzer nefesh ha'am: literally 'the soul/spirit of the people became short' — katzer (short, cut short) applied to nefesh (soul/spirit/life). The idiom for impatience, frustration, discouragement: the spirit becomes 'too short' for the burden of the journey. The circuitous route around Edom shortens Israel's spiritual patience.
5: vayedaber ha'am be'Elohim uveMoshe lamah he'elitunu mimitzrayim lamut bamidbar ki ein lechem ve'ein mayim venafshenu katzah balachem hakelokelt: 'the people spoke against God and against Moses: Why have you brought us up out of Egypt to die in the wilderness? For there is no food and no water, and we loathe this worthless food.' Venapshenu katzah balachem hakelokelt: 'our soul loathes/is disgusted with this contemptible food.' Kelokelt (contemptible/miserable/light) — a dismissive term for the manna. The people who once complained there was no food now despise the food YHWH provides. The escalation of complaint is notable: rejection of the gift itself.
6: vayeshalach YHWH ba'am et hanechashim hasrafim vayyinashu et-ha'am vayamot am-rav miYisrael: 'YHWH sent fiery serpents among the people, and they bit the people, so that many people of Israel died.' Nechashim srafim (fiery/burning serpents): saraf means both 'burn' and is related to seraphim (the burning/fiery ones of Isaiah 6:2). The serpent sent as divine judgment on the manna-despising generation bites with fire. The connection to Eden's serpent is unmistakable: the original instrument of the fall returns as the instrument of covenant judgment.
7: vayavo ha'am el-Moshe vayomru chatanu ki-dibarnu veYHWH uvach hitpalel el-YHWH veyasar me'alenu et-hanachash vayitpalel Moshe ba'ad ha'am: 'the people came to Moses and said: We have sinned, for we have spoken against YHWH and against you. Pray to YHWH, that he take away the serpents from us. So Moses prayed for the people.' Chatanu (we have sinned) — the sin is named. The pattern of judgment-confession-intercession-provision recurs from Numbers 11 (Taberah, v1-3) and Numbers 16 (Korah plague, Numbers 16:46-48). Moses's intercession is immediate and unrecorded — the prayer is brief; the provision is elaborate.
9: vaya'as Moshe nachash nechoshet vayesimehu al hanes vehayah im-nasach hanachash et-ish vehibit el-nachash hanechoshet vachai: 'Moses made a bronze serpent and set it on a pole. And if a serpent bit anyone, he would look at the bronze serpent and live.' The fulfillment of the look-and-live provision. Al hanes (on the pole/standard): the nes (pole/sign/banner/miracle) on which the serpent is raised is the same word used for military standards and miracles throughout Numbers. The image raised on the pole — which brings healing to those who look — becomes the explicit type Jesus names: 'As Moses lifted up the serpent in the wilderness, so must the Son of Man be lifted up, that whoever believes in him may have eternal life' (John 3:14-15).
10: vayisu vnei Yisrael vayachanu be'Ovot: 'the people of Israel set out and camped at Oboth.' The journey resumes after the bronze serpent episode. The itinerary list (vv10-20) preserves the route through Transjordan with various stopping points. The names encode the wilderness experience: Ovot may connect to ov (spirit/ghost/necromancer) — the wilderness place-names often carry etymological memory.
11: vayisu me'Ovot vayachanu be'Iye-ha'Avarim bamidbar asher al-penei Moav mimizrach hashemesh: 'they set out from Oboth and camped at Iye-abarim, in the wilderness that is opposite Moab, toward the sunrise.' Iye-ha'Avarim (ruins of the Abarim/crossings): the Avarim (those who cross over) are mentioned — this region borders the Jordan crossing territory. Mimizrach hashemesh (toward the sunrise) — the east/sunrise orientation marks progress toward Canaan.
12: misham nas'u vayachanu benachal Zared: 'from there they set out and camped in the Valley of Zered.' Nachal Zered (Wadi Zered) — the traditional border between Edom/Moab territory; crossing the Zered marks a significant geographical transition in the wilderness journey (Deuteronomy 2:13-14 identifies crossing the Zered as the moment the military generation finished dying).
13: misham nas'u vayachanu me'ever ha'Arnon asher bamidbar hayotze migvul ha'Emori ki Arnon gvul Moav bein Moav uvein ha'Emori: 'from there they set out and camped on the other side of the Arnon, which is in the wilderness that extends from the boundary of the Amorites. For the Arnon is the boundary of Moab, between Moab and the Amorites.' Nachal Arnon (the Arnon River/valley) — the border between Moab (south) and the Amorite kingdom of Sihon (north). The journey is now in the territory that Israel will need to traverse by defeating Sihon (vv21-32).
14: al ken ye'amar besefer milchamot YHWH et-Vaheb beSufah venathalim Arnon: 'therefore it is said in the Book of the Wars of YHWH: Waheb in Suphah, and the valleys of the Arnon.' Sefer milchamot YHWH (Book of the Wars of YHWH) — an ancient Israelite source text now lost; cited here for the geographical/military record. The existence of this book suggests that Numbers draws on archival sources; it preserves a fragment of early Israelite military poetry.
15: ve'eshed hanechalim asher natah leshevet Ar venishaan ligvul Moav: 'and the slope of the valleys that extends to the seat of Ar, and leans to the border of Moab.' The geographical detail of the journey route; the archival fragment continues with place-names along the Arnon valley.
16: umiSham Be'erah hi habe'er asher amar YHWH leMoshe esof et-ha'am ve'etenah lahem mayim: 'and from there they continued to Beer; that is the well about which YHWH said to Moses: Gather the people together, so that I may give them water.' Beer = well. YHWH provides water again — this time without the Meribah failure; the provision is gracious, the response is poetic.
17: az yashir Yisrael et-hashira hazot ali ve'er enu-lah: 'then Israel sang this song: Spring up, O well! — sing to it!' Az yashir (then sang) — the same introduction as the Song of the Sea (Exodus 15:1). This brief well-song is Israel's second wilderness song. Ali ve'er (spring up, O well): the well is addressed directly as if it has agency. Paul later identifies the wilderness water-source as Christ: 'they drank from the spiritual Rock that followed them, and the Rock was Christ' (1 Corinthians 10:4).
18: be'er charuha sarim kafruha nedivei ha'am bimechoqek bemish'anotam: 'a well that the princes dug, that the nobles of the people delved, with the scepter and with their staffs.' The leaders dig the well; the mechoqeq (scepter/lawgiver) and mish'anot (staffs) are used as digging tools. The imagery of rulers digging the well is unusual; it may encode the tradition of Moses striking the rock (now transformed into collaborative well-digging) or it may be a literal memory of a communally dug cistern.
19: umiMidbar Matanah umiMatanah Nachaliel umiNachaliel Bamot: 'and from the wilderness to Mattanah, and from Mattanah to Nahaliel, and from Nahaliel to Bamoth.' The place-names are themselves theological: Mattanah = gift; Nachaliel = river/stream of God; Bamot = high places. The journey passes through places named Gift → River of God → High Places: a theological topography.
20: umiVamot hagei asher bisadeh Moav rosh hapisgah venishkafah al-penei hayeshimon: 'and from Bamoth to the valley in the region of Moab by the top of Pisgah that overlooks the desert.' Rosh hapisgah (the top of Pisgah) — this peak will appear again in the Balaam oracles (Numbers 23:14) and as Moses's death location (Deuteronomy 34:1). The itinerary positions Israel at the height overlooking the Jordan valley — the land is visible from here.
21: vayishlach Yisrael mal'achim el-Sichon melech ha'Emori lemor: Israel sends messengers to Sihon king of the Amorites. The diplomatic protocol mirrors the Edom request (Numbers 20:14-21): the same language, the same King's Highway, the same promises. But Sihon is not a brother-nation — the Amorites have no covenantal claim against Israel's passage.
22: e'evrah ve'artzecha lo nitteh besadeh uvecherem lo nishteh mei be'er derech hamelech nelech ad asher na'avor gvulecha: 'let me pass through your land. We will not turn aside into field or vineyard. We will not drink the water of a well. We will go by the King's Highway until we have passed through your territory.' The identical diplomatic formula used with Edom — word for word. The comparison is deliberate: Israel makes the same request, but Edom (a brother) and Sihon (an Amorite) both refuse. The Amorite refusal, unlike Edom's, leads to war.
23: velo-natan Sichon et-Yisrael avor bigvulo vaye'esof Sichon et-kol-ammo vayetze likrat Yisrael hamilbarah: 'but Sihon did not allow Israel to pass through his territory. He gathered all his people together and went out against Israel to the wilderness.' Hamilbarah (to the wilderness/desert) — Sihon comes out to attack in the open desert terrain. Deuteronomy 2:30 adds: 'YHWH your God hardened his spirit and made his heart obstinate, that he might give him into your hand.' Sihon's refusal is the divinely-ordained trigger for Israel's first major land victory.
24: vayakkehu Yisrael lefi-charev vayirash et-artzo miArnon ad-Yabok: 'Israel struck him with the edge of the sword and took possession of his land from the Arnon to the Jabbok.' Lefi-charev (at the mouth of the sword) — the sword's edge expressed as its mouth (peh, the same word as mouth for speech and water-giving). Vayirash (took possession) — the verb of covenant inheritance: Israel takes possession (yarash) of the first Transjordanian territory. The Arnon to Jabbok boundary becomes Israelite territory.
25: vayikach Yisrael et kol-he'arim ha'eleh vayeshev Yisrael bekol-arei ha'Emori beCheshbon uvechal-banotehah: 'Israel took all these cities, and Israel settled in all the cities of the Amorites, in Heshbon, and in all its villages.' Vayeshev (settled/dwelt) — Israel inhabits the Amorite cities. This is the first settlement in territory east of the Jordan — the beginning of the conquest pattern.
26: ki Cheshbon ir Sichon melech ha'Emori hi vehu nilcham bemelech Moav harishon vayikach et-kol-artzo miyado ad-Arnon: 'for Heshbon was the city of Sihon the king of the Amorites, who had fought against the former king of Moab and taken all his land from him as far as the Arnon.' Historical context: Heshbon had been Moabite before Sihon conquered it. Israel conquers what was Moab's former territory through Sihon — an indirect inheritance through conquest.
27: al-ken yomru hamoshlim bo'u Cheshbon tibbaneh vetikonen ir Sichon: 'therefore the ballad singers say: Come to Heshbon, let it be built; let the city of Sihon be established.' A poetic fragment from the moshlim (those who compose proverbs/songs) celebrating Sihon's former victory — which Israel now inherits. The taunt song against Moab (vv27-30) was originally a celebration of Sihon's conquest of Moab; Israel now sings it as their own victory song, having defeated Sihon.
28: ki esh yatze'ah miCheshbon lehabah mir'kiryat Sichon achlah Ar Moav ba'alei bamot Arnon: 'for fire came out from Heshbon, flame from the city of Sihon. It devoured Ar of Moab, and swallowed the heights of the Arnon.' The taunt poem: fire/flame imagery for military conquest. Esh... lehabah (fire... flame) — two terms for the unstoppable advance. Moab's destruction by Sihon (now inherited by Israel's victory) is described in fire-language that resonates with the divine fire motif throughout Numbers.
29: oy-lecha Moav avadeta am Kemosh natan banav pelitim uvenotav bashevut lemelech Emori Sichon: 'woe to you, O Moab! You are undone, O people of Chemosh! He has made his sons fugitives, and his daughters captives, to an Amorite king, Sihon.' Chemosh — Moab's national deity. The taunt names Chemosh as the failed divine protector: 'his sons became fugitives... to an Amorite king.' YHWH defeats the nations by defeating their gods. Oy-lecha (woe to you) — the prophetic woe oracle applied in taunt poetry.
30: vanniram avad Cheshbon ad-Divon vanavashim ad-Nofa asher ad-Medeivah: 'we overthrew them; Heshbon, as far as Dibon, perished; and we laid waste as far as Nophah, which reaches to Medeba.' The conclusion of the taunt song. The place names — Dibon, Nophah, Medeba — mark Moabite territory now claimed by Israel's advance. The song ends with comprehensive destruction.
31: vayeshev Yisrael be'eretz ha'Emori: 'Israel settled in the land of the Amorites.' The settlement verb (vayeshev) repeats from v25: the conquest becomes habitation. The land east of the Jordan is now Israelite territory, claimed by victory over Sihon.
32: vayishlach Moshe laragel et-Yazer vayilkedu et-benotehah vayorish et-ha'Emori asher-sham: 'Moses sent to spy out Jazer, and they captured its villages and dispossessed the Amorites who were there.' Laragel (to spy out) — a small reconnaisance that contrasts with the catastrophic spying of Numbers 13; this time, the spying leads immediately to capture, not despair.
33: vayifnu vaya'alu derech haBashan vayetze Og melech haBashan likratam hu vekhol-ammo lamilchamah beEdre'i: 'they turned and went up by the way of Bashan. And Og the king of Bashan came out against them, he and all his people, to battle at Edrei.' Og of Bashan — the giant king (Deuteronomy 3:11: his iron bed was 9 cubits long) whose kingdom extends north of Gilead. His coming out against Israel repeats Sihon's pattern.
34: vayomer YHWH el-Moshe al-tira oto ki veyad'cha natattihu ve'et-kol-ammo ve'et-artzo: 'YHWH said to Moses: Do not fear him, for I have given him into your hand, and all his people, and his land.' Al-tira (do not fear) — the divine assurance before battle. YHWH's word precedes the victory: the outcome is given before the fight. The same formula recurs throughout Joshua (Numbers 1:9; Numbers 10:8) and becomes the foundation for Israel's conquest confidence.
35: vayakku oto vet-banav vekhol-ammo ad-bilti hish'ir-lo sarid vayirshu et-artzo: 'and they struck him and his sons and all his people, until he had no survivor left. And they possessed his land.' Ad-bilti hish'ir-lo sarid: 'until no survivor remained' — the totality of the cherem-pattern victory. Vayirshu (they possessed) — the same possession-verb as v24. The double conquest of Sihon and Og east of the Jordan establishes the Transjordanian foothold from which Joshua will launch the main Canaan campaign. Moses's sermon in Deuteronomy returns to these two victories as the paradigm for Israel's faith in YHWH's conquest promises.
